content.text: React apps are built from components, and those components live in separate files. JavaScript's ES module system —
— is what ties them together. Getting these conventions right prevents silent bugs (e.g., importing a component that resolves to
ES modules give you two ways to expose values from a file.

attrs.alt: Named export vs default export syntax comparison

content.text: . A file can have any number of named exports.
Callers must use the exact name in curly braces, or rename with

import { formatDate, formatCurrency } from './utils/formatters';
import { formatDate as fmtDate } from './utils/formatters';
Exactly one default export per file. The importer can choose any name.
Mixing both in the same file
A file can have one default export and multiple named exports simultaneously:
React doesn't enforce a naming scheme, but every mainstream project follows one consistently.
extension signals JSX syntax; Vite and Create React App handle it automatically. Some teams use
for TypeScript components. Either is fine as long as the project is consistent.
The strong convention in React codebases is one primary component per file. This isn't enforced by the language, but it has practical benefits:
File name matches component name — you find
Shorter diffs — changes to one component don't mix with unrelated ones.
Easier code splitting — bundlers can split at file boundaries.
Helper subcomponents used only inside a parent are sometimes colocated in the same file. That's fine as long as only the parent file exports them.
As projects grow, two common patterns emerge for organizing component folders.

src/
  components/
    Button.jsx
    UserCard.jsx
    Modal.jsx
    Navbar.jsx
  pages/
    Home.jsx
    Dashboard.jsx
Works well for small to medium projects. Easy to find any component at a glance.

src/
  components/
    Button/
      index.jsx       ← component
      Button.test.jsx ← tests
      Button.css      ← styles
    UserCard/
      index.jsx
      UserCard.test.jsx
      UserCard.module.css
All files for one component live together. The
file lets callers import with just the folder name:

// Works because Button/index.jsx is the folder entry point
import Button from './components/Button';
that re-exports from multiple files in its folder, giving callers a single import point:

barrel files defeat tree-shaking in some bundler configurations because importing any one export forces the bundler to analyse all exports. In large apps, prefer direct imports or ensure your bundler's tree-shaking handles barrels correctly (Vite and modern webpack do in most cases).
By default, imports are relative paths starting with

// Relative — fragile when files move
import { api } from '../../../utils/api';
Path aliases resolve this. In Vite, configure
When a named export is imported without braces, you get
silently — the component just doesn't render.
Adding braces to a default export import
without explicit extensions by default. Node.js ESM does not — you need the full
extension. Stick to the extension convention your toolchain uses.
If file A imports from file B and file B imports from file A, you have a circular import. JavaScript handles this by returning an incomplete module — some exports will be
at runtime. Restructure so shared code lives in a third file that neither A nor B depends on.
Named vs Default: When to Use Which
There is no absolute rule, but these guidelines reflect mainstream React practice:
Callers import exactly what they need
Explicit; hooks often live in single files per hook
Multiple values from the same source
Callers control what they pull in
Re-exports let you change the public surface of a module without moving files. Useful in barrel files and when adapting third-party shapes.
For performance-critical paths, ES modules also support dynamic imports that load on demand. React wraps these with
Dynamic imports work with both default and named exports, though
requires the import to resolve to a module with a default export.
Putting It Together: A Real Project Layout
Here is how the conventions above combine in a mid-size Vite/React project:

src/
  components/           ← shared UI components
    Button.jsx           ← default export: Button
    Modal/
      index.jsx          ← default export: Modal
      Modal.test.jsx
    index.js             ← barrel: re-exports Button, Modal
  hooks/
    useLocalStorage.js   ← named export: useLocalStorage
    useFetch.js          ← named export: useFetch
    index.js             ← barrel: re-exports all hooks
  pages/
    Home.jsx             ← default export: Home
    Dashboard.jsx        ← default export: Dashboard
  utils/
    formatters.js        ← named exports: formatDate, formatCurrency
    validators.js        ← named exports: isEmail, isPhone
  App.jsx                ← root component, default export
  main.jsx               ← entry point (no exports)
) enforces a consistent import ordering that most React teams adopt:

// Well-ordered imports
import { useState } from 'react';          // 1. framework
import { motion } from 'framer-motion';    // 2. third-party
import { Button } from '@/components';     // 3. internal absolute
import { formatDate } from '../utils';     // 4. relative
import './Dashboard.css';                  // 5. styles
In TypeScript projects, you import types and interfaces the same way as values, but the
syntax is preferred for type-only imports. It lets the bundler erase the import entirely at build time without running the module.
You can import all named exports from a module into a single namespace object using

Namespace imports are useful when you need many exports from the same module, but they prevent tree-shaking because the bundler can't determine statically which members you'll use. Prefer named imports for application code.
Some modules are imported purely for their side effects — they don't export anything you reference directly. CSS and polyfill files are the common case:

Test files sit next to the components they test and import from them directly. Barrel files are intentionally not used in test imports — tests should target the specific module, not the aggregated barrel, so that a bug in
When a component grows complex enough to move into its own folder, the import path changes. The
alias makes this cheaper — callers don't need to update their own import paths if the component's public alias doesn't change:

Before:
  src/components/Modal.jsx
  imported as: import Modal from '@/components/Modal'

After (moved into folder):
  src/components/Modal/index.jsx
  imported as: import Modal from '@/components/Modal'  ← unchanged!

Because Node/bundler resolution: Modal → Modal/index.jsx automatically.
No callers need to be updated when moving from a flat file to a folder-with-index — the import path resolves identically either way.
use curly braces at import; callers must match the exported name. Good for utility files with multiple exports.
have no curly braces; callers choose the local name. One per file. Preferred for components.
) re-export from a folder for cleaner import paths, with a trade-off on tree-shaking.
) eliminate deep relative paths and are worth setting up early.
uses dynamic imports for on-demand loading of large components.
